learning_objectives: Apply the communication process model to business contexts
Identify barriers to effective communication in organizations
Adapt communication style to audience and purpose

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective communication is the most consistently cited skill employers seek. This course develops your ability to write, speak, and present in professional business contexts.
key_concepts: Communication process: sender, encoding, channel, decoding, receiver, feedback
Barriers: noise, filtering, selective perception, information overload, jargon
Audience analysis: demographics, psychographics, knowledge level, needs
Communication styles: direct vs. indirect approaches
Channel selection: when to email, call, meet, or present
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audience analysis exercise: adapting the same message for three different audiences (CEO, technical team, external client)
setup_requirements: Textbook: Locker & Kienzler, Business Communication (13th ed.)

review.value: Start with the audience's needs, knowledge, and concerns; frame every message in terms of what matters to THEM, not you
Face-to-face is richest (nonverbal cues, immediate feedback); use richer channels for complex, ambiguous, or sensitive messages

class_schedule.topic: Professional Writing: Emails, Memos, and Reports
learning_objectives: Write clear, concise, and professional business emails
Structure business reports for executive audiences
Apply the inverted pyramid and BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front) techniques

lecture_notes.introduction: In business, writing is action. Every email, memo, and report either advances your goals or undermines your credibility. Clear, purposeful writing is a professional superpower.
key_concepts: BLUF: state the conclusion first, then support it
Minto Pyramid Principle: group ideas logically in pyramid structure
Email etiquette: subject lines, length, tone, action items
Report structure: executive summary, findings, analysis, recommendations
Editing for clarity: active voice, short sentences, precise word choice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Before-and-after revision: rewriting a poorly structured business email and a rambling one-page memo into clear, professional communications

review.value: Bottom Line Up Front: lead with the most important information (conclusion, recommendation, or request) so busy readers get the key message immediately
Structure ideas from the top down: main point first, then supporting arguments grouped logically, then detailed evidence for each argument

learning_objectives: Construct persuasive arguments using logos, ethos, and pathos
Design proposals that address stakeholder objections proactively
readings: Fisher & Ury, Getting to Yes (key concepts)

lecture_notes.introduction: Much of business communication is persuasion: convincing stakeholders to approve a project, accept a recommendation, or change behavior. Effective persuasion is ethical, evidence-based, and audience-centered.
key_concepts: Aristotle's appeals: logos (logic), ethos (credibility), pathos (emotion)
Monroe's Motivated Sequence: attention, need, satisfaction, visualization, action
Handling objections: acknowledge, reframe, evidence, redirect
Principled negotiation: separate people from problem, focus on interests not positions
Written proposals: problem statement, proposed solution, cost-benefit, implementation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Persuasive pitch exercise: presenting a business recommendation to skeptical stakeholders, handling live objections

review.value: Focus on mutual interests rather than positions; invent options for mutual gain; use objective criteria to evaluate solutions
Attention (grab interest), Need (establish problem), Satisfaction (present solution), Visualization (show impact), Action (call to action)

learning_objectives: Design presentation slides that support (not replace) the speaker
Apply storytelling techniques to business presentations
Deliver presentations with confidence, clarity, and presence

lecture_notes.introduction: A great presentation moves people to action. It combines compelling content, effective visuals, and confident delivery. Most business presentations fail because they prioritize information transfer over audience engagement.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: hook, context, body (3 key points), conclusion, call to action
Slide design: one idea per slide, minimal text, powerful visuals
Data visualization in presentations: simplify, highlight, explain
Delivery skills: eye contact, vocal variety, purposeful movement, pausing
Handling Q&A: bridging, acknowledging, and redirecting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Presentation makeover: transforming a text-heavy 20-slide deck into a compelling 12-slide visual presentation
setup_requirements: Presentation software (PowerPoint, Keynote, or Google Slides)

review.value: Slides support the speaker; they should not be a document projected on screen. If you can email your slides and skip the talk, your slides have too much text.
Structure presentations as narratives: establish the challenge (tension), present the solution (resolution), and inspire action (transformation)

learning_objectives: Translate data analysis into business narratives
Avoid common pitfalls in data presentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Data without narrative is noise. The ability to turn complex analysis into clear, actionable stories is what separates analysts who influence decisions from those who produce ignored reports.
key_concepts: Storytelling with data: context, action, result framework
Choosing the right chart: comparison, composition, distribution, relationship
Eliminating clutter: Tufte's data-ink ratio applied to business charts
Executive communication: 1-page summaries, dashboards, elevator pitches
Common mistakes: misleading scales, cherry-picking data, correlation vs. causation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data story exercise: taking a raw dataset, identifying the key insight, and creating a one-page executive summary with supporting visualization

review.value: Set context (why should the audience care), present the data (what happened), explain the 'so what' (implications), recommend action (what should we do)
One page that answers: what is the issue, what did we find, what do we recommend, what are the next steps

learning_objectives: Develop crisis communication plans and holding statements
Adapt communication for cross-cultural business contexts

lecture_notes.introduction: The true test of business communication comes under pressure: crises that demand rapid response, cultural differences that create misunderstanding, and difficult conversations that most people avoid.
key_concepts: Crisis communication: speed, transparency, empathy, accountability
Crisis response framework: acknowledge, apologize, act, advance
Cross-cultural communication: adapting to high/low context cultures
Difficult conversations: SBI model (Situation, Behavior, Impact)
Digital communication challenges: tone in email, social media crises
lecture_notes.demonstration: Crisis communication simulation: responding to a product safety issue, drafting press statement, internal memo, and social media response under time pressure

review.value: Respond within the first hour with facts you know, empathy for those affected, and commitment to investigation; silence is interpreted as guilt or indifference
Situation (when and where), Behavior (what specifically was said/done), Impact (the effect on you/others/the business); avoids judgment and focuses on observable facts

questions.question: You need to email your VP requesting approval for a $50,000 training budget. The VP is data-driven and skeptical of spending. Write the email subject line and first two sentences using the BLUF approach.
questions.answer: Subject: Request for Approval: $50K Training Investment Projected to Reduce Turnover by 15%. First two sentences: 'I am requesting approval for a $50,000 employee training program that is projected to reduce department turnover by 15%, saving approximately $180,000 annually in replacement costs. The program targets the three skill gaps identified in our Q3 exit interview analysis as primary reasons for voluntary departures.' This leads with the ask and the ROI, addressing the VP's data-driven nature and spending skepticism immediately.

rubric.key: Clear subject line with key information
BLUF opening with recommendation and justification

questions.question: A colleague submits a proposal that buries the recommendation on page 8, starts with extensive background, and uses passive voice throughout. Explain three specific changes that would improve it using course principles.
questions.answer: (1) Move the recommendation to page 1: apply the Pyramid Principle by leading with the conclusion, then supporting arguments, then evidence. Executives read the first page; if the recommendation is on page 8, many will never reach it. (2) Add a one-page executive summary: summarize the problem, recommendation, key supporting evidence, cost/benefit, and next steps on a single page before the detailed proposal. (3) Convert passive voice to active voice: 'It was determined that costs could be reduced' becomes 'We can reduce costs by 15% through...' Active voice is clearer, more direct, and more persuasive because it assigns accountability.

questions.question: Your presentation slides have 8 bullet points per slide with full sentences, no images, and a company logo taking up 20% of each slide. Identify three problems and how to fix each.
questions.answer: (1) Too much text: 8 bullet points with full sentences means the audience reads ahead instead of listening. Fix: reduce to 1-3 key phrases per slide or split into multiple slides with one concept each. Use speaker notes for detail. (2) No visual support: text-only slides are the least engaging format. Fix: replace text with relevant images, diagrams, or data visualizations that illustrate the point; the speaker provides the explanation verbally. (3) Logo consuming valuable space: a large logo on every slide wastes 20% of your visual real estate and adds no information after the title slide. Fix: use a small, subtle logo or remove it from content slides entirely; place it only on the title and closing slides.

questions.question: You need to present a 12% decline in quarterly revenue to the executive team. Describe how you would frame this data story using the situation-complication-resolution structure.
questions.answer: Situation: 'We entered Q3 with strong momentum from two consecutive quarters of 8% growth, driven by our new product line and expanded distribution.' Complication: 'Q3 revenue declined 12%, driven primarily by two factors: a 20% drop in our enterprise segment due to delayed purchasing decisions amid economic uncertainty, and supply chain disruptions that delayed $2.3M in orders past quarter-end.' Resolution: 'We have three actions underway to recover: (1) accelerating the delayed enterprise deals with modified payment terms, (2) securing a backup supplier for the disrupted component, and (3) launching a Q4 promotion targeting our highest-margin products. Based on current pipeline, we project Q4 revenue to recover to Q2 levels.' This framing is honest about the decline while showing the leadership team's understanding of causes and proactive response.

rubric.key: Honest about the decline without being defeatist

major_assignments.title: Professional Communication Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Build a portfolio of professional business communications including a persuasive proposal, executive presentation, crisis communication response, and data-driven report. Each piece must demonstrate audience analysis, clear structure, and professional quality.
objectives: Demonstrate proficiency across multiple business communication formats
Apply audience analysis to adapt messages for different stakeholders
Create professional-quality written and visual communications
Develop a portfolio usable for job applications
deliverables: Executive presentation deck (15 slides) with speaker notes
Crisis communication response package (press release, internal memo, social media posts)
Data-driven one-page executive summary with visualization

criteria: All three formats appropriate for their audience
Strong persuasive proposal with audience-centered approach
Professional presentation design and compelling delivery
Effective crisis communication across multiple formats
Clear data-driven executive summary with visualization

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of communication principles and application

class_participation.description: Active engagement in workshops, peer reviews, and presentation exercises

midterm_examination.description: Covers writing, persuasion, and presentation principles

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including data communication, crisis, and cross-cultural

course_policies.attendance: Mandatory for workshops and presentation days. Peer review exercises contribute to participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Portfolio components penalized 10% per day. Presentation must be delivered on assigned date.
course_policies.collaboration: All portfolio pieces are individual work. Peer review feedback is collaborative and expected.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All writing must be original. Using AI tools for drafting must be disclosed; final work must be substantially your own.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Presentation software, word processor, and data visualization tool. Recording capability for self-review of presentations.
